questions.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client who has end-stage kidney disease (ESKD) and reports having shortness of breath and swelling in his lower extremities. Upon assessment, the nurse notes the client has crackles in his lungs and an elevated blood pressure. The nurse should suspect which of the following based on the client's manifestations?
questions.correctAnswer: Hypervolemia

questions.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client who has a new arteriovenous (AV) graft in his left forearm. Which of the following techniques should the nurse use to assess the patency of this graft?
options: Measure the client's blood pressure to ensure it is higher in the left arm than the right.
Check the brachial and radial pulses of the left arm simultaneously.
Auscultate the site for a bruit.
Auscultate the antecubital fossa using a Doppler stethoscope.
questions.correctAnswer: Auscultate the site for a bruit.
questions.explanationText: Auscultate the site for a bruit, then palpate for a thrill.

questions.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client who is undergoing initial peritoneal dialysis. Which of the following should the nurse report immediately to the provider?
options: Report of discomfort during dialysate inflow
Dialysate fluid is slow to drain out.
questions.correctAnswer: Purulent dialysate outflow
questions.explanationText: Peritonitis infection risk leading to sepsis risk (a life-threatening body-wide response to infection that causes organ dysfunction.)

questions.questionText: A nurse is reviewing the medication record for a client who has chronic kidney disease. Which has potential to cause nephrotoxicity?
questions.correctAnswer: Vancomycin.
questions.explanationText: Vancomycin and Metformin are major concerns

questions.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client who is receiving peritoneal dialysis. The nurse should monitor the client for which of the following manifestations of peritonitis?
questions.correctAnswer: Nausea and vomiting.
questions.explanationText: Peritonitis Signs: ✅ Nausea and vomiting ✅ Abdominal pain/tenderness ✅ Fever ✅ Malaise ✅ Cloudy or purulent dialysate outflow (most important finding) ✅ Hypoactive bowel sounds

questions.questionText: A nurse is teaching a client who has pre-dialysis end-stage kidney disease about diet. Which of the following instructions should the nurse include?
options: Eliminate foods high in protein from your diet.
Reduce intake of foods high in potassium.
questions.correctAnswer: Reduce intake of foods high in potassium.

questions.questionText: A nurse is assessing a child who has nephrotic syndrome. Which of the following findings should the nurse expect?
questions.correctAnswer: Facial edema.

questions.questionText: A nurse is reviewing the laboratory test results from a client who has prerenal acute kidney injury (AKI). Which of the following electrolyte imbalances should the nurse expect?
questions.correctAnswer: Hyperkalemia.

questions.questionText: Client has acute kidney injury. Teach the oliguric phase.
options: Urine output is less than 400 mL per 24 hr.
questions.correctAnswer: Urine output is less than 400 mL per 24 hr.

questions.questionText: 6-year-old client has nephrotic syndrome. Which adverse effects of corticosteroids has potential to impact child's psychosocial development?
questions.correctAnswer: Weight gain, Irritability.

questions.questionText: What is prerenal kidney injury?
options: Reduced systemic circulation, i.e. severe blood loss, low BP, sepsis, injury
Direct damage to  renal tissue, i.e. med toxicity, ischemia, prolonged dehydration/sepsis.
Mechanical obstruction of urinary outflow, i.e. prostatic hypertrophy (enlarged prostate)
questions.correctAnswer: Reduced systemic circulation, i.e. severe blood loss, low BP, sepsis, injury

questions.questionText: What is intrarenal kidney injury?
options: Reduced systemic circulation, i.e. severe blood loss, low BP, sepsis, injury
Direct damage to  renal tissue, i.e. med toxicity, ischemia, prolonged dehydration/sepsis.
Mechanical obstruction of urinary outflow, i.e. prostatic hypertrophy (enlarged prostate)
questions.correctAnswer: Direct damage to  renal tissue, i.e. med toxicity, ischemia, prolonged dehydration/sepsis.

questions.questionText: What is postrenal kidney injury?
options: Reduced systemic circulation, i.e. severe blood loss, low BP, sepsis, injury
Direct damage to  renal tissue, i.e. med toxicity, ischemia, prolonged dehydration/sepsis.
Mechanical obstruction of urinary outflow, i.e. prostatic hypertrophy (enlarged prostate)
questions.correctAnswer: Mechanical obstruction of urinary outflow, i.e. prostatic hypertrophy (enlarged prostate)

questions.questionText: What type of AKI is this? Man fell off roof, severe lacerations and blood loss
What are the 4 phases of acute kidney injury?
questions.correctAnswer: Initiating, Oliguric, Diuretic, Recovery

questions.questionText: What happens in oliguric phase of AKI?
options: Oliguria <400 mL/day, occurs within 1-7 days of kidney injury, metabolic acidosis, hyperkalemia, hyponatremia, elevated BUN, elevated creatinie, fatigue, malaise
Gradual increase in urine output, may reach 3-5 L/day, hypovolemia, dehydration, hypotension, BUN and Creatine Levels beigin to normalize
Begins when GFR increases, BUN and Creatine Levels Plateau, then drop
questions.correctAnswer: Oliguria <400 mL/day, occurs within 1-7 days of kidney injury, metabolic acidosis, hyperkalemia, hyponatremia, elevated BUN, elevated creatinie, fatigue, malaise

questions.questionText: What happens in diuretic phase of AKI?
options: Oliguria <400 mL/day, occurs within 1-7 days of kidney injury, metabolic acidosis, hyperkalemia, hyponatremia, elevated BUN, elevated creatinie, fatigue, malaise
Gradual increase in urine output, may reach 3-5 L/day, hypovolemia, dehydration, hypotension, BUN and Creatine Levels beigin to normalize
Begins when GFR increases, BUN and Creatine Levels Plateau, then drop
questions.correctAnswer: Gradual increase in urine output, may reach 3-5 L/day, hypovolemia, dehydration, hypotension, BUN and Creatine Levels beigin to normalize

questions.questionText: What happens in recovery phase of AKI?
options: Oliguria <400 mL/day, occurs within 1-7 days of kidney injury, metabolic acidosis, hyperkalemia, hyponatremia, elevated BUN, elevated creatinie, fatigue, malaise
Gradual increase in urine output, may reach 3-5 L/day, hypovolemia, dehydration, hypotension, BUN and Creatine Levels beigin to normalize
Begins when GFR increases, BUN and Creatine Levels Plateau, then drop
questions.correctAnswer: Begins when GFR increases, BUN and Creatine Levels Plateau, then drop

questions.questionText: Which TYPE if kidney injury is this? J.K.’s injuries have been repaired, and seemed that he was ready to go home, but his urine output has dropped below 350 mL/day.  J.K.’s laboratory results reveal BUN 53 mg/dL and serum creatinine 2.7 mg/dL. His BP has risen to 178/98. The pharmacist reports a critically high. Vancomycin level in his bloodwork. JK received Vancomycin to prevent infection due to his injuries.
questions.correctAnswer: Intra-renal

questions.questionText: Which PHASE of kidney injury is this? J.K.’s injuries have been repaired, and seemed that he was ready to go home, but his urine output has dropped below 350 mL/day.  J.K.’s laboratory results reveal BUN 53 mg/dL and serum creatinine 2.7 mg/dL. His BP has risen to 178/98. The pharmacist reports a critically high. Vancomycin level in his bloodwork. JK received Vancomycin to prevent infection due to his injuries.
questions.correctAnswer: Oliguric
questions.explanationText: Low urine output, BUN and Creatinine trending up.  Also, metabolic acidosis, hyperkalemia, hyponatremia, fatigue, malaise

questions.questionText: What TREATMENT do you anticipate? J.K.’s injuries have been repaired, and seemed that he was ready to go home, but his urine output has dropped below 350 mL/day.  J.K.’s laboratory results reveal BUN 53 mg/dL and serum creatinine 2.7 mg/dL. His BP has risen to 178/98. The pharmacist reports a critically high. Vancomycin level in his bloodwork. JK received Vancomycin to prevent infection due to his injuries.
options: hold vancomycin, monitor BUN/creatinine, monitor electrolytes, monitor urine output, manage fluid balance, restrict fluids if fluid overloaded, control blood pressure, treat hyperkalemia if present, consider dialysis if severe complications develop
questions.correctAnswer: hold vancomycin, monitor BUN/creatinine, monitor electrolytes, monitor urine output, manage fluid balance, restrict fluids if fluid overloaded, control blood pressure, treat hyperkalemia if present, consider dialysis if severe complications develop

questions.questionText: When do we do dialysis
options: We tried diuretics. Volume overload, Elevated serum potassium level, Metabolic acidosis, BUN level > 120 mg/dL (43 mmol/L), Significant change in mental status, Pericarditis, pericardial effusion, or cardiac-tamponade, Clinical status of patient
questions.correctAnswer: We tried diuretics. Volume overload, Elevated serum potassium level, Metabolic acidosis, BUN level > 120 mg/dL (43 mmol/L), Significant change in mental status, Pericarditis, pericardial effusion, or cardiac-tamponade, Clinical status of patient

questions.questionText: A client on a medical unit has a K+ level of 6.8 mEq/L. What is the priority action that the nurse should take?
options: Place the client on a cardiac monitor.
Teach the client to avoid high-potassium foods.
Call the lab and request a redraw of lab to verify the results.
questions.correctAnswer: Place the client on a cardiac monitor.

questions.questionText: Top causes of AKI (acute kidney injury)
questions.correctAnswer: Dehyration, Nephrotoxic Drugs

questions.questionText: Top causes of CKD (chronic kidney disease)
questions.correctAnswer: Diabetic Nephropathy, Hypertension

questions.questionText: Vitals for a CKD (chronic kidney disease) Patient
options: BP is Hypertensive, SpO2 is lower, RR is higher, HR is normal or increased, Temp is normal
questions.correctAnswer: BP is Hypertensive, SpO2 is lower, RR is higher, HR is normal or increased, Temp is normal
questions.explanationText: Hypervolemia could lead to high BP, pulmonary edema (fluid in lungs),

questions.questionText: Biggest difference between AKI and CKD (chronic kidney disease)
options: CKD is irreversable.  NOTE: GFR is gold standard lab.
questions.correctAnswer: CKD is irreversable.  NOTE: GFR is gold standard lab.

questions.questionText: Uremia, Oliguria, Anuria
options: Uremia=waste buildup in blood, Oliguria=low urine output, Anuria=no urine output
questions.correctAnswer: Uremia=waste buildup in blood, Oliguria=low urine output, Anuria=no urine output

questions.questionText: Which serum laboratory value indicates to the nurse that the client’s CKD is getting worse?
questions.correctAnswer: Decreased calculated glomerular filtration rate (GFR)

questions.questionText: During the nursing assessment of the patient with renal insufficiency, the nurse asks the patient specifically about a history of?
questions.correctAnswer: Hypertension.

questions.questionText: The nurse teaches a patient with chronic kidney disease about prevention of complications. What should the nurse include in the teaching plan?
options: Monitor for proteinuria daily with a urine dipstick.
Perform self-catheterization every 4 hours to measure urine.
Take calcium-based phosphate binders on an empty stomach.
Check weight daily and report a gain of greater than 4 pounds
questions.correctAnswer: Check weight daily and report a gain of greater than 4 pounds

questions.questionText: What are the methods of dialysis?
questions.correctAnswer: Peritoneal, Hemodialysis
questions.explanationText: Peritoneal cavity is space in abdomen located between peritoneum's parietal and visceral layers (membrane that lines the abdominal cavity and covers many abdominal organs).  Hemodialysis via AV fistula (forearm area), AV graft (forearm area), central venous cath

questions.questionText: Hemodialysis Complications
questions.correctAnswer: Assess for hypotension (hypovolemia)
